summary: Jeff Atwood responds to critics who dismissed background compilation as a feature only for beginners and hacks. He argues that just as background spell checking has become universally beloved and enabled by default, background compilation offers the same unobtrusive benefits for catching common coding errors. Atwood rejects the notion that superior developers should eschew helpful tools, pointing out that for every Linus Torvalds there are ten thousand ordinary programmers who benefit from better tooling. He confesses his own 'neanderthal' blog writing setup as evidence that refusing better tools isn't noble—it's counterproductive. The post concludes that while these tools aren't substitutes for critical thinking, intentionally avoiding them is both patronizing and bad business.
key_points: Background compilation and background spell checking serve the same purpose: unobtrusive, automatic error detection that users can ignore or act upon
The 'infinite monkey software development model' is reality—most programmers aren't Linus Torvalds, and tools that help average developers represent real advancement
Deliberately avoiding helpful tools to prove expertise is counterproductive and often just bad business
Tools should never have the last say, but that's an argument for understanding limitations, not abandoning tools entirely
Background spell checking is beloved enough to be a default feature in major software—background compilation deserves the same acceptance
money_quotes: The infinite monkey software development model is what we're stuck with.
For every one Linus Torvalds, there are ten thousand programmers who aren't Linus Torvalds.
I see very little difference between background spell checking and background compilation.
Using the most primitive of tools to compose these blog posts isn't a glorious, sweeping validation of my expertise as a writer. If anything, it's an indication of my idiocy.
Any reasonably competent person knows that tools are never substitutes for critical thinking about what you're writing or coding.
Intentionally choosing not to use better tools because you're afraid they will become a crutch is, at best, cruel and patronizing.
key_insight: Rejecting helpful development tools to prove your expertise isn't a sign of mastery—it's an unwillingness to let better tools help you be better at your craft.
